exhibits.name: Housing Crisis Timeline
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT A - HOUSING CRISIS TIMELINE

June 30, 2025: Plaintiff fled his shared housing situation due to escalating harassment and intimidation by housemate Respondent. Initial refuge at colleague's empty house with 11-day time limit.

July 1-11, 2025: Temporary housing at colleague's house while seeking permanent solution through university channels.

July 12-21, 2025: Housing uncertainty intensifies as temporary arrangement nears expiration.

July 21, 2025: Explicit homelessness warning sent to senior university leadership (Dean A, Faculty A, Legal Affairs): "I fled my home at substantial cost in order to protect my own physical safety…. I can remain at Colleague C's empty house for 11 more days. I do not have a permanent place. I cannot go to Germany as I planned and received an award for, due to all this financial and logistical disruption."

July 22, 2025: University acknowledgment of homelessness warning but no immediate action.

July 24, 2025: Dean Wasilko formally denies housing assistance despite available resources: "I heard back from housing, and it looks like right now the only rooms we have are in the first year buildings. Obviously, that is not helpful at this juncture." (Exhibit G)

July 25-August 12, 2025: Plaintiff forced into precarious couch-surfing arrangements while university maintains denial despite having confirmed housing availability.

August 13, 2025: Plaintiff submits Emergency Removal Request seeking Title IX supportive measures including housing. (Exhibit B-5A)

August 13-14, 2025: Simpson's 24-hour jurisdiction flip - initially accepts Title IX complaint, then explicitly disclaims jurisdiction over sex discrimination complaints. (Exhibits B-5, B-6)

August 15, 2025: Sawa threatens disciplinary charges for seeking help from university officials. (Exhibit B-7)

August-October 2025: Continued housing instability as all university channels fail to provide federally mandated supportive measures. "Out of couches to surf" - housing crisis reaches critical phase with "imminent complete homelessness."

Medical documentation confirms: "He reports homelessness since June 30th which has been very stressful and likely worsens his mental health conditions. I recommend all measures to protect him from his abuser and to provide stable housing be made." Treating Physician. (Exhibit F)

Present: 90+ days of homelessness while university maintains available housing resources but refuses compliance with federal Title IX supportive measures requirements.

exhibits.name: Initial Request for Emergency Housing
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT B-0: INITIAL REQUEST FOR EMERGENCY HOUSING
June 30, 2025

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
To: Dr. Adam Wasilko <[redacted]>
Cc: Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>, Faculty A <Faculty A>

Dear Dean Wasilko,

I'm writing to report an incident that occurred this evening involving my roommate, Respondent, which left me feeling physically threatened and unsafe in my own home.

While I am already in the process of filing a small claims complaint related to the housing situation, this escalation was entirely unexpected and extremely distressing.

Respondent approached me in the kitchen; when I calmly stated that I was not interested in talking, she began yelling at me, got within inches of my face, and aggressively followed me through the apartment. When I asked if she was following me, she confirmed it aloud. She pursued me up the stairs toward my room, saying "I'm coming up," and only stopped just short of entering. I locked myself in my room and remained there, visibly shaken. I even told her I was recording—for my own safety—though I was not, because I felt that threatened.

I no longer feel safe sharing a living space with her. I am urgently seeking any emergency housing options, protective campus measures, or relevant reporting mechanisms available. I am also requesting that your office formally document this event and advise on next steps, including whether this constitutes a violation of university conduct standards.

Please let me know how to proceed. I am happy to provide a full written statement or speak further at any time.

Sincerely,
David Mullins

From: Dr. Adam Wasilko [redacted]
Date: Tue, Jul 1, 2025 at 8:49 AM
To: David Mullins [redacted]
Cc: Dr. Daniel Selcer [redacted], Faculty A
Subject: Re: Urgent Safety Concern and Housing Support Request

Hi David—

Thanks for reaching out. I am sorry you are dealing with this!

Are you looking for on campus housing options in the interim?

Your best option would be to file a police report through our public safety. You can do that by calling [redacted] or by walking into the police station on campus at any time. They are there 24/7. They would then share the report to see if conduct charges were applicable.

I certainly understand that the situation sound unsettling, but it doesn't sound like a code of conduct violation at this point. It is always best to document these things in case they escalate.

Dr. W

Dr. Adam T. Wasilko
Associate Vice President for Student Life
Dean of Students
Duquesne University
[redacted]

EXHIBIT B-0A: GENDER REVERSAL HYPOTHETICAL
July 23, 2025

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
Date: Wed, Jul 23, 2025 at 5:39 AM
To: Dr. Adam Wasilko <[redacted]>
Cc: Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>, Faculty A <Faculty A>
Subject: Re: Safety Concerns Regarding Respondent

Dear Dean Wasilko,

Respectfully, I did not file a report with Public Safety because, frankly, I did not trust your guidance. Your initial response minimized a serious and dangerous situation and displayed a fundamental misunderstanding of the student conduct code. I concluded that (1) a formal City of Pittsburgh police report would carry more weight and credibility than a campus safety report, and (2) there is no plausible or good-faith reading of the account I provided in my initial email (quoted in full below) that could justify your conclusion that it "doesn't sound like a code of conduct violation." That language profoundly undermined my confidence in your role as an advocate for student safety and Title IX compliance. In retrospect, I can't help but wonder whether your response would have been the same had the genders in this situation been reversed.

Housing — I didn't respond about housing because I was able to find something temporary, but I'm glad you brought it up again: I may not have a place or the funds for a deposit from the beginning of August until I've received two paychecks in September. If assistance is still available, I would greatly appreciate it.

All my best,
David

Dear David,

My name is Alicia Simpson and I am the Title IX Coordinator and Director of Sexual Misconduct Prevention and Response for Duquesne University. Also copied on this email is Dr. Wasilko, as I know you have previously communicated with her regarding your concerns.

My office is notified when there are reported incidents of alleged sexual harassment or sexual misconduct involving students, faculty and staff. I am in receipt of your email where you allege that there has been noncompliance with Title IX regarding a situation with another student that has caused you to be displaced from your housing. I am sorry to hear of the difficult situation you have been experiencing.

One of my primary roles is to help ensure your health and safety. The Title IX office provides oversight to the University processes for Title IX sexual harassment and non-Title IX sexual misconduct, and I am here to provide you with support and talk with you about resources on our campus and in the community.

Based on the nature of this report, I would like to meet with you to discuss the report, gather additional information, go over your options under our policies, and discuss any supports or resources you may need. Please reply to this email with your availability if you would like to meet with me.

At this time, based on the information I have been provided, I do not have enough information to determine whether the appropriate policy to address this report is TAP 31, TAP 61, or our Student Code of Conduct. Once we meet and I am able to gather additional information, I will determine the appropriate policy and let you know.

I look forward to hearing from you.

Thank you,
Alicia

exhibits.name: OCR Escalation and Records Demand
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT B-3A: OCR ESCALATION AND RECORDS DEMAND
July 26, 2025

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
To: president@duq.edu
Cc: Alicia Simpson <[redacted]>, provost@duq.edu, Faculty A <Faculty A>, Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>, legal@duq.edu, Dr. Adam Wasilko <[redacted]>, OCR.Atlanta@ed.gov, OCR@ed.gov
Date: Sat, Jul 26, 2025 at 6:06 AM
Subject: Title IX Retaliation, Ongoing Housing Crisis—Immediate Referral to Office for Civil Rights (OCR)

Dear University President, Senior Leadership, and All Relevant Offices,

I am writing to notify you that effective immediately, I am referring my case to the US Department of Education Office for Civil Rights. This escalation is a direct result of the University's ongoing failure to provide Title IX-mandated protection and the recent act of retaliation and obstruction by Dean Adam Wasilko on July 25, 2025.

SUMMARY OF VIOLATIONS:

Failure to act on a clear Title IX safety threat. My report of stalking, intimidation, and credible fear for my safety by a female roommate was minimized, not referred to appropriate channels, and not investigated as required. I believe this is chiefly because I am male. Had I been female and my roommate male, the response would have been immediate and protective.

Ongoing housing insecurity. After fleeing my home on June 30th out of fear for my physical safety, I remain without stable, graduate-appropriate housing as a direct and ongoing result of the University's inaction. As of today, I have less than $40 in my bank account, no permanent housing, and no credible path towards it.

Retaliation and obstruction. After my first demand for immediate remedy (Morning July 24th), Dean Wasilko sent a reply that attempted to deflect, silo, and pressure me into further delay. He removed senior leadership from the CC chain and failed to provide any meaningful resolution. This constitutes ongoing noncompliance and retaliation under Title IX.

Complicity of Administration, Including Local Title IX Office. Since Dean Wasilko's retaliatory email was sent with both the Executive Director of Student Conduct and the Title IX Coordinator on copy, and neither responded with an offer of meeting, remedy, or even acknowledgment, it is now clear to me that the entire infrastructure responsible for student safety and civil rights at Duquesne is complicit in this ongoing abuse.

I REITERATE MY DEMANDS FOR REMEDY:
- Graduate-appropriate housing or fully funded off-campus housing through Fall 2025, effective immediately
- Official retroactive Title IX designation for the June 30 incident
- Written acknowledgment of Title IX and reporting failures by Dean Wasilko
- Immediate production of all records, reports, and communications related to my case
- $20,000 in compensation for administrative labor, emotional distress, academic disruption, and housing insecurity

Sincerely,
David Mullins

exhibits.name: Plaintiff Meeting Refusal & OCR Restoration
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT B-5A: PLAINTIFF MEETING REFUSAL & OCR RESTORATION
August 13, 2025

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
Date: Wed, Aug 13, 2025 at 4:20 PM
To: Alicia Simpson <[redacted]>
Cc: David Mullins <[redacted]>, Public Safety Officer A <Public Safety Officer A>,
    Public Safety Officer B <Public Safety Officer B>, Dr. Anne Mullarkey Sawa
    <[redacted]>, OCR@ed.gov, legal@duq.edu, provost@duq.edu,
    president@duq.edu, Faculty A <Faculty A>,
    Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>, OCR.Atlanta@ed.gov

Title IX and Public Safety,

I am not available for meetings (Zoom or otherwise). Please handle this in writing only.

OCR is copied; do not remove OCR, Legal Affairs, senior leadership, Public Safety, or departmental leadership from the CC line going forward.

Deadline

Please provide written confirmation of Option A or Option B by 5:00 PM ET today (August 13, 2025).

Reason for the deadline

Given the ongoing safety risk, the prior documentation (see Exhibit I), and the fact that supportive measures are administrative directives that can be implemented immediately (email orders to Public Safety/Registrar/Access Control; written notice from Title IX), a 5:00 PM ET deadline is appropriate for a prompt response. If you cannot finalize the full package by 5:00 PM ET, please by 5:00 PM ET: (1) issue the mutual no-contact and no-presence-in-shared-spaces orders, (2) place the Public Safety CAD alert and identify a duty contact, and (3) confirm registrar separation. The remaining items (building-access flags, mailbox reassignment, written risk analysis or emergency-removal notice) should follow within 2 hours.

Regulatory framework (for reference)

  34 C.F.R. §106.44: respond promptly and not be deliberately indifferent.

  34 C.F.R. §106.30: supportive measures are non-disciplinary, non-punitive,
  individualized, offered as appropriate and as reasonably available, without fee or
  charge, designed to restore or preserve access and protect safety, without
  unreasonably burdening the other party.

  Coordinator duty: supportive measures are available with or without a formal
  complaint.

  34 C.F.R. §106.44(c): emergency removal may follow an individualized safety
  and risk analysis finding an immediate threat to physical health or safety, with
  notice and an opportunity to challenge immediately following removal.

  34 C.F.R. §106.71: retaliation is prohibited.

Required written action — today

Please complete one of the following and confirm in writing by 5:00 PM ET:

Option A — Emergency removal (physical presence only)
  1. Issue an emergency physical-presence ban for the respondent from all campus
     properties and events (remote participation preserved).
  2. Provide the written individualized safety/risk analysis and the respondent's
     opportunity-to-challenge language.

Option B — Full supportive-measures set (implemented now)
  - Mutual no-contact order.
  - Space/course separation: registrar-enforced sectioning; Graduate Lounge
    exclusion; reassigned mailbox/building access.
  - Public Safety: CAD alert, priority-response protocol, and a named point of
    contact.
  - Modality fallback (remote) for me until all measures are live; return to normal
    in-person access thereafter.
  - All communications in writing via Title IX (no meetings required).

Record and notice

I have provided documented proof of homelessness and a negative bank balance and have repeatedly requested supportive measures since June 30. Conditioning supportive measures on additional meetings is inconsistent with the duty to offer and implement them promptly, including with or without a formal complaint. Given the record (see Exhibit I)—including prior removals of OCR, Legal Affairs, and senior leadership from CC—further conditioning or delay is likely to be viewed as retaliatory and will be reported to OCR (cc'd).

Delivery and confirmation

If you decline emergency removal, please provide the written risk analysis and the complete list of supportive measures implemented today. Send same-day written confirmation of Option A or Option B to all parties on this thread, including OCR, Legal Affairs, senior leadership, Public Safety, the DGS, and the Department Chair.

— David Mullins

Attachments / references:
  Exhibit I — Meetings Record & Administrative Non-Response (timeframe now
  extended through Aug. 13, 2025).
  Exhibit I — Meetings Record & Administrative Non-Response-1.pdf  108K

exhibits.name: Simpson Jurisdiction Disclaimer
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT B-6: SIMPSON JURISDICTION DISCLAIMER
August 14, 2025

From: Alicia Simpson <[redacted]>
Date: Thu, Aug 14, 2025 at 9:42 AM
To: David Mullins <[redacted]>
Cc: Public Safety Officer A, Public Safety Officer B, Dr. Sawa

Dear David,

The Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety procedures all involve a request for a meeting so that we are able to ensure that we have gathered all relevant information prior to determining the appropriate University policy and/or process to address a reported concern. I understand that at this time, you are unwilling to meet with us to discuss the report and your concerns. Without a meeting, I am limited to reviewing the information you provided in order to make a preliminary determination, outlined below, regarding the Title IX Office's jurisdiction over the reported situation.

The Title IX Office is solely responsible for addressing reports of alleged violations of the University's Title IX (TAP 61) policy and the University's non-Title IX sexual misconduct (TAP 31) policy. Reported matters that are outside the scope of TAP 61 or TAP 31, will not be addressed by my office and will instead be referred to the appropriate University department.

With regard to your reported allegations against your former roommate, Respondent, based on a review of the information you have provided, I have determined that the reported alleged conduct does not meet TAP 61, nor TAP 31's definitions of prohibited conduct, even if proven to be true. Therefore, based on information provided by you at this time, the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested.

If you wish to pursue allegations of disparate treatment sex discrimination against Dr. Adam Wasilko, those allegations also are outside the jurisdiction of the policies (TAP 61 and TAP 31) administered by the Title IX Office. Instead, I would refer you to TAP 30, which establishes the University's policy prohibiting unlawful discrimination in the classroom and workplace.

My determinations are based on the information provided by you as of today's date and are subject to change upon receipt of additional information that alleges violations of our TAP 61 or TAP 31 policies. Accordingly, I remain available to meet with you should you wish to do so in the future.

Sincerely,
Alicia

DECLARATION OF EXTERNAL FACULTY WITNESS

I, External Faculty Witness, pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1746, declare under penalty of perjury:

1. I am a Professor of Political Science at the University of Hawai'i at Manoa and have long served as an academic mentor to David Mullins.

2. Based on my knowledge of David's academic work and communications, his education and research have been severely derailed by his housing instability and the conflict with his former roommate.

3. In my opinion, supportive housing and protective measures are necessary for him to resume his academic work.

Executed on 9-30-2025.

External Faculty Witness
Professor, Department of Political Science
Director, University of Hawai'i Research Center for Futures Studies
The University of Hawai'i at Manoa

SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATION OF EXTERNAL FACULTY WITNESS
RE: SEPTEMBER 17, 2025 CONVERSATIONS WITH DAVID MULLINS

BACKGROUND AND RELATIONSHIP

1. I previously submitted a declaration on September 30, 2025, regarding the severe educational harm David Mullins has suffered due to housing instability and lack of university support.

2. In addition to my role as David's academic mentor, I am also a personal friend and someone David contacts regarding both academic matters and significant personal events affecting his studies.

DAVID'S PRIOR EXPRESSIONS OF FEAR

1. In conversations prior to September 17, 2025, David had explained to me his fear of encountering his former roommate, Respondent, on campus.

2. He described the June 30, 2025 incident where Respondent pursued him to his bedroom after he stated he didn't feel safe, and his ongoing anxiety about encountering her without protective measures in place.

3. David had expressed frustration that university officials were forcing his in-person return beginning September 15, 2025, without implementing any protective measures such as no-contact orders or registrar-enforced separation.

4. He specifically warned that he feared encountering Respondent in academic spaces and that such encounters would cause severe distress and prevent his educational participation.

SEPTEMBER 17, 2025 INITIAL CONTACT

1. On September 17, 2025, at approximately 6:51 p.m., David contacted me via Facebook Messenger.

2. David reported that Respondent had been waiting outside his Philosophy seminar classroom.

MY FOLLOW-UP AND DAVID'S RESPONSE

1. Recognizing the significance of this encounter given David's prior expressed fears, I asked him how he was doing.

2. Several hours later, David responded "better now."

3. David's delayed response and the content of his message ("better now" rather than "I'm fine") made me concerned that the encounter had been more serious than his initial brief report suggested.

MY ASSESSMENT BASED ON THESE CONVERSATIONS

1. As someone who has known David in both academic and personal contexts for 19 years, I understood this communication pattern to reflect genuine distress.

2. This incident is directly related to the educational harm I described in my September 30 declaration: David cannot focus on his PhD research when he is experiencing encounters that cause this level of distress and require hours of recovery time.

AUTHENTICATION OF FACEBOOK MESSAGES

1. I have reviewed the Facebook Messenger screenshots attached as Exhibit C and confirm they accurately reflect the conversations David and I had on September 17, 2025.

2. The timestamps shown in those screenshots correspond to my recollection of when David initially contacted me and when he responded to my follow-up inquiry.

I declare under penalty of perjury that the foregoing is true and correct.

Executed on October 1st, 2025, in Huntsville, Alabama.

External Faculty Witness, PhD
Professor of Political Science
University of Hawai'i at Mānoa

exhibits.name: Signal Transcript & Timeline
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT D: SIGNAL TRANSCRIPT & TIMELINE
June 23-30, 2025

TIMELINE SUMMARY:

June 2 – Move-in at [street address] with co-tenant Respondent.

June 13–16 – Respondent initiates repeated discussions about A/C use ahead of the upcoming heat wave, asking multiple times if setting the thermostat to 78°F is acceptable and if Mullins is comfortable with the cost. Mullins agrees and says comfort is the priority.

June 19–20 – Heatwave begins. Temperature in Mullins' bedroom and office rises to the high 80s, peaking at 90°F. Mullins raises concerns about being unable to work or sleep due to heat and dehydration. Respondent claims the A/C cannot cool the house further, citing poor insulation, high outside temperature, risk of malfunction if set lower, cost, risk of power blackouts, and her prior experience. Mullins requests clarification; Respondent confirms that lowering the thermostat would not be effective.

June 21–25 – Mullins repeatedly communicates ongoing heat distress; Respondent suggests drinking more water or Gatorade.

June 25 – Respondent requests lowering the A/C for her own comfort. The apartment immediately becomes livable at the new setting. She offers no comment regarding her previous claims.

June 26 – Mullins raises concerns about the inconsistency in a Signal chat, states that the A/C will remain on, and insists on transparency regarding the utility bill and an end to further manipulation. Respondent requests an in-person meeting; Mullins does not respond, preferring written communication. He ceases all direct in-person contact.

August 25, 2025

From: Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>

To: David Mullins <[redacted]>

Dear David,

Thank you for meeting with me and Faculty A last Thursday via Zoom.

You explained that you are housing insecure, and that your first priority is to find housing. You indicated the department community has been supportive and that people have offered to give you a place to stay if you cannot find anything. We hope you are continuing to make progress on this front.

You also shared that your second priority is to be back physically in the classroom. We explained that the expectation in our program is that seminar participation is in-person, and that TA responsibilities require presence in class.

You said that you want to have particular safety measures in place before you will come on campus. You explained that you do not feel safe on campus because you fear Respondent. You confided that you feel physically intimidated by her, and that you believe she has an unregistered firearm.

You categorized the safety measures that you want as: 1. A no-contact order; 2. A campus safety order (a public safety escort when you arrive on campus), and 3. Registrar separation.

We explained that the Philosophy Department could not issue the orders that you are requesting, and that you would need to follow the procedures of the potentially relevant university offices (Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety) in order to seek these measures.

In the meantime, as remote TA work is not viable and at this point and you are not willing to come to campus, I am reassigning you for the FA25 semester to Faculty B, for whom you will work virtually as a Research Assistant. Your duties will be outlined in a subsequent communication. This remote work for your stipend is permitted during the FA25 semester only - in SP26 you will again be assigned as a TA and expected to attend the class in which you work in person.

You are also temporarily authorized to remotely attend the two classes in which you are enrolled this semester - the Graduate Teaching Seminar and Marion & Phenomenology. This is possible only because they are two of the very few graduate classes we are running this semester in which room and class size permits remote participation.

This remote seminar attendance for these FA25 classes is authorized only for the first three weeks of class at this time. It is also authorized subject to the condition that you fully engage with the Title IX, Student Conduct, and Public Safety offices within the next week and beyond, consistent with their prior instructions to you regarding next steps. If you are requesting a medical accommodation, it is also conditioned on you fully engaging with the Disability Services Office consistent with their processes.

All my best,
Dan

---

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
Date: Mon, Aug 25, 2025 at 10:06 PM
To: Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>
Cc: David Mullins <[redacted]>, Faculty A <Faculty A>, Dean A <Dean A>, OCR.Atlanta@ed.gov

Dear Dan,

I want to begin by noting that I have tried very hard to engage Duquesne's processes in good faith. But at the end of the day, the law is clear: federal obligations take precedence over internal procedures.

First, supportive measures are my right under Title IX and ADA, not contingent on my compliance with further "process." I have already engaged extensively in writing, provided documentation including a physician's letter, sworn statement from me and an eyewitness, and escalated to OCR oversight.

Second, the three-week limit you have placed on remote participation is neither neutral nor legitimate. It acknowledges that accommodations are feasible but withholds them as a temporary concession, creating yet another crisis point after months of ongoing crisis.

Third, I want to emphasize that I have never refused to come to campus. My consistent position is that I am able and willing to attend in person if appropriate safety measures are implemented.

Fourth, the measures I requested, a no-contact directive, registrar separation, and a campus safety escort, are baseline supportive measures under Title IX. They are non-punitive and designed solely to preserve access.

Fifth, your summary omits the fact that the Title IX Office informed me in writing that "the Title IX Office does not have jurisdiction over your report and cannot take action or provide assistance as requested." This determination was issued without any legal basis.

Sixth, your summary also omits that the head of Student Conduct threatened me that if I continued emailing University officials about my safety and access, she would initiate conduct charges against me. I immediately identified this as retaliatory under 34 C.F.R. §106.71.

I am also CC'ing OCR on this communication and ask that they be included on all further correspondence regarding this matter.

Sincerely,
David Michael Mullins

exhibits.name: Mullins Demands Retraction of Retaliatory Zoom Countdown
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT I-1: MULLINS DEMANDS RETRACTION OF RETALIATORY ZOOM COUNTDOWN
September 2, 2025

From: David Mullins <[redacted]>
To: Dr. Daniel Selcer <[redacted]>
CC: David Mullins <[redacted]>, Faculty A <Faculty A>, Dean A <Dean A>, OCR.Atlanta@ed.gov
Date: Sep 2, 2025, 2:59 AM
Subject: Re: follow up to 8/21/25 meeting

Dear Dan,

I have received no clarity or response to my correction of the record. I wish to reiterate two central points:

1. Remote access is not a choice and does not fulfill the University's obligations.

While I am grateful to have Zoom access at present, it does not resolve the core failure: I remain denied meaningful contact with colleagues and faculty in person, use of departmental offices, and access to the library. These are all integral to my education. My in-person access is not a matter of preference but of institutional denial: it is the administration's refusal to provide basic safety measures that has foreclosed my ability to participate in campus life. Title IX and ADA require supportive measures regardless of jurisdictional findings or dismissal of a complaint. The philosophy department cannot unilaterally provide these measures; only central administration can. But at a minimum, the department could have avoided retaliating against me—and could retract that retaliatory posture now.

2. The "countdown" limitation on Zoom is retaliatory and unlawful.

The imposition of a three-week cap is not a supportive measure at all. It is a countdown clock designed to coerce compliance or force attrition, a textbook example of retaliation masquerading as accommodation. Federal regulations are explicit:

• §106.44(g)(2): "No punitive or disciplinary measures."
• §106.44(g)(4): "Right to seek impartial review of supportive measure decisions."

The threat itself is harmful. Every day the countdown remains, I have to wonder whether my access will suddenly be cut off. By its own terms, the threat suggests I have already "violated" the condition, since I cannot and will not "re-enter" a process that has threatened and abandoned me. This leaves me opening my inbox uncertain whether I will be forced back to campus against medical directives, destabilizing my education and health.

The department must commit to my right to safe access to education. In practice, this means securing my Zoom access indefinitely until central administration addresses my safety concerns. Anything less leaves my access contingent, uncertain, and retaliatory. Even if central administration do not do so until forced to by OCR, the department's federal obligation to protect my access remains. If this results in my being on Zoom for too long, that is unfortunate for me but is the fault of central administration. The department can still show it is more concerned with rights, federal compliance, and my safety than with the looming threat of accidentally setting a remote access precedent for the program.

If the department does not intend to follow through on this retaliatory threat but leaves it in place, then it is inflicting harm pointlessly: every day the threat remains live, I must anticipate being cut off. If the department does intend to follow through, then it is openly retaliating — and must, at minimum, provide me the impartial review required under §106.44(g)(4).

No precedent is set here — remote access is a remedial measure in a singular case of Title IX and ADA failure. And of course, any worry about precedent is no alibi for federal noncompliance: the department's fear is not just cruel in its consequences but a massive source of liability.

Accordingly:
• The three-week limit is facially retaliatory and must be retracted.
• Even if it is not retracted, I must be given the opportunity to challenge any termination of my Zoom access before an impartial decision-maker, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,
David Michael Mullins

EXHIBIT N: FACULTY D SYLLABUS/POLICY (ANYONE CAN ZOOM)
PHIL 520–420W – Religious Platonism
Spring 2025 Semester

COURSE INFORMATION:
PHIL 520–420W – T 3:05–5:45pm – CH324
FACULTY D OFFICE HOURS: T 1-3pm – CH 328

ATTENDANCE POLICY (KEY ZOOM PROVISION):
Since this class meets only once a week, you should plan to attend every class. Although we will meet in person, I will also keep a Zoom meeting running, so if you are not feeling well, please attend that session remotely! If you need to attend a session remotely, please email me ahead of time to let me know. If there is more than one week when you are not present in some form (in-person or remotely), you will need to write up a minimum 700 word paper discussing something from the text for the week you were absent, or lose one-third of a letter grade on your course grade. The paper is due within seven days of the day you are absent.

COURSE DESCRIPTION:
We will look at the aesthetic, ethical, and metaphysical consequences generated by the irruption of popular religion into the Greek philosophical tradition, such that philosophers came to be regarded as saints. We will begin with Porphyry’s embrace of popular religion as analogous to philosophy, his canonization of his teacher Plotinus, and his dialogue with Iamblichus, who in turn systematized the analogy between philosophy and popular religious practice. We will then spend most of the term on the restless fourth- and fifth-century Neoplatonist Augustine, focusing particularly on his great psychological treatise, the Confessions, and his primary hermeneutical treatise, On Christian Teaching.

REQUIRED TEXTS:
Neoplatonic Philosophy: Introductory Readings, edited by John Dillon and Lloyd P. Gerson (Hackett, 2004); Saint Augustine, Confessions, trans. Henry Chadwick (Oxford University Press, 1992). Saint Augustine, On Christian Teaching, trans. R.P.H. Green (Oxford University Press, 2008 [1997]).

PRESENTATIONS:
Each graduate student will sign up for one five-minute presentation on anything covered in class the previous week. The presentation will be based on (but will not consist in simply reading) a paper of 1000–2000 words (including footnotes) submitted to the Canvas site by midnight Sunday night. Together your paper, presentation, and questions for other presenters constitute 30% of your course grade.

TERM PAPER:
The term paper, due before midnight on 4/30, should be no less than 5000 words in length for graduate students, have a strong thesis, and (for graduate students) make use of relevant secondary literature. The paper should not have a bibliography. All citations should be in Chicago style footnotes. Your term paper constitutes 70% of your course grade.

ACADEMIC INTEGRITY:
Plagiarism—including quoting or nearly quoting someone else’s words without acknowledgment, or using an artificial intelligence program to compose portions of your work for you—will result in a failing grade for the course.

Let me be clear: I told you and am telling you I fear for my safety. Not in the past, but now and in the future. I fled my home at substantial cost in order to protect my own physical safety. I was harassed and followed as I was leaving (\"predator vibes,\" said Colleague C, who helped me move out): \"Who you talking to David?\" \"Where you going David?\" Respondent following me up and down the stairs as I moved out, despite me repeatedly saying, \"I don't want to talk, I don't feel safe.\"

As I said in my previous email, every time I have pursued a process or set a boundary, Respondent has retaliated. I am filing in small claims court. I am talking to you. When she learns that I even tried to pursue formal redress, I will be in danger. \"You F****** talked to the landlord?!\" was what she yelled as she followed me through our house and started the stairs to our bedroom.

I can remain at Colleague C's empty house for 11 more days. I do not have a permanent place. I cannot go to Germany as I planned and received an award for, due to all this financial and logistical disruption.

Do you think I have ever made a university or legal complaint in my life? I haven't. This is the first and only time. Respect me enough to presume its legitimacy, at least enough to read closely all I present to you.

I want to be clear that this is not a matter I take lightly. It needs to be taken seriously or I will be more than happy to pursue avenues of litigation. Let's please not do that.

What should we do? I am open to additional ideas, but there is one that is clearly necessary.

I request assistance navigating and drafting an account of the clear and multiple student conduct violations in this situation. When the Dean Wasilko said it \"didn't reach the level of [a violation]\" in their email, they were clearly and obviously wrong. In about 5 minutes skimming the handbook I found the below rules, *all* of which I am sure I could demonstrate either apply to this case or they apply nowhere:

**Relevant Student Conduct Violations**

1. **Bullying:**
Use of written, verbal, or electronic expression, or physical acts/gestures, likely to cause emotional or physical harm, intimidate, control, or diminish another person physically or mentally.

2. **Coercion:**
Use of force, threat, or emotional intimidation to cause or attempt to cause another person to engage in or submit to certain activities.

3. **Endangering Health or Safety of Another Person:**
Taking or threatening action that endangers the safety, physical or mental health, or life of any person, and/or creating a reasonable fear of such action.

4. **Incivility through Language or Actions:**
Engaging in disruptive, ill-mannered, disrespectful, or offensive behavior.

5. **Stalking:**
Repeatedly following, harassing, threatening, or intimidating a specific person to the extent that it causes reasonable fear for their safety or substantial emotional distress, by any means (in person, electronic, social media, etc.).

6. **Disorderly or Disruptive Conduct:**
Conduct which unreasonably interferes with university or community activities or with the legitimate activities of any university member, including disruption or obstruction of teaching, research, administration, meetings, or community living.

7. **Dishonesty and Misrepresentation:**
Failing to maintain a high standard of integrity and truthfulness in actions, including:
a) Misrepresenting personal information to university officials.
b) Forgery or unauthorized use/alteration of documents or IDs.

8. **Violating Laws and/or Policies:**
Violating any published university policy, rule, regulation, guideline, procedure, and/or any federal, state, or local law or ordinance.

9. **Failure to Behave in Keeping with the University's Mission:**
Engaging in behavior that is not consistent with the university's mission.

Once I receive confirmation that I will be allowed to pursue this process as is my right, I will be happy to share the small claims file I submitted to court, which documents in irrefutable detail, including chat transcripts, e-mails, the police report and the officer's own assessment (\"Someone this deranged should not be in a graduate program\"), the bullying, manipulative, and cruel behaviors of Respondent relative to me.

Please reply with a formal written response outlining next steps and confirming my right to pursue these violations through the university's conduct process.

My only goal is a just, safe, and fair resolution; I hope it will not require further escalation. I don't like writing emails in this tone; I am from Texas and my grandmother is probably already complaining in paradise about my manners.

All my best,

David

EXHIBIT X-6: SELCER/MULLINS MEETING — NO PERFORMANCE ISSUES LANGUAGE (CONDENSED)
February 10, 2026
Zoom meeting between Dr. Daniel Selcer (Chair) and David Mullins

CONTEXT: Five days after Faculty B emailed Mullins asking him not to come to class (Exhibit X-4), Selcer meets with Mullins to discuss the situation. No "performance issues" language appears anywhere in this meeting. Three days later, Selcer's written reassignment letter (Exhibit X-8) introduces the phrase for the first time.

KEY EXCHANGE 1 — SELCER'S FRAMING:
Selcer: "I understand there have been some issues between you and Faculty B, and I am trying to understand the situation and want to give you the opportunity to give me your perspective on what happened."

KEY EXCHANGE 2 — MULLINS REPORTS NO PRIOR COMPLAINTS:
Mullins: "This is the first I'm hearing of any problem or issue of any kind. Faculty B has been nothing but enthusiastic about my work, both last semester's research assistant, and this one."
Mullins: "I've consistently fulfilled every research and TA responsibility requested — attending lectures, meeting deadlines — without issue or complaint until now."

KEY EXCHANGE 3 — FACULTY B'S POSITIVE FEEDBACK CONFIRMED:
Mullins: "She was a difficult supervisor, but I've made it work and we made it work and she said nothing but positive things about me is how I experienced it. If there's been some running issue, I haven't heard about it. I've heard just positive stuff."

KEY EXCHANGE 4 — FACULTY B DOES NOT CONTROL STIPEND:
Mullins: "Am I at a risk of being instantaneously fired?"
Selcer: "Well, we don't instantaneously fire people, David. A faculty supervising a student TAing under a stipend — that is a supervisory relationship, but it's not the kind of formalized supervisory relationship that would have something to do with the status of a stipend. Faculty B doesn't make decisions about your stipend."
Mullins: "So how to proceed is up in the air, but my stipend—"
Selcer: "Let me just figure out what the actual situation is and we will speak again."

KEY EXCHANGE 5 — SELCER'S COLLEGIAL TONE:
Selcer asks Mullins to skip one class session while he interviews everyone: "It gives me a chance to talk to everyone."
Selcer on the seminar withdrawal: "I would hope that no matter who's involved, professional issues with respect to TA work are different from faculty-student relationships and seminars."

PATTERN: On Feb 10, Selcer frames this as a situation he needs to understand, uses no "performance issues" language, confirms Faculty B has no stipend authority, and maintains a collegial tone. Three days later (Exhibit X-8), he introduces "performance issues raised by two of the three faculty members," threatens stipend suspension through 2029, and questions Mullins's viability as instructor of record.

exhibits.name: Deliberate Indifference Standard — Training Definition
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT Z-b: DELIBERATE INDIFFERENCE STANDARD — TRAINING DEFINITION
PCHE Summer 2020 Title IX Training (Connelly)

Slide 5 — Three-Part Framework
1. Actionable sexual harassment PLUS
2. Actual knowledge triggers a school's response which
3. Must not be deliberately indifferent

Slide 64 — Required Response
Where a school has: (1) had actual notice; (2) of sexual harassment; (3) in a program or activity; (4) in the U.S., it must respond:
- Promptly (to include undefined timeframes for all steps)
- In a manner that is not "deliberately indifferent"
- Equitably, including explaining and providing supportive measures
- By adopting and following new procedures to include a "Formal complaint" process, prior to issuing any discipline
- By adopting a grievance process that includes a presumption that the Respondent is "not responsible for the alleged conduct" until a determination is made regarding responsibility at the end of the process

Slide 65 — What is Deliberate Indifference?
A school is deliberately indifferent ONLY where:
- Its response to sexual harassment is clearly unreasonable under the known circumstances
- If the school follows the procedural requirements, the OCR will not find deliberate indifference solely because it would have come to a different conclusion

PRACTICE POINTER: You should not be striving to merely satisfy the "deliberate indifference" standard

exhibits.name: Supportive Measures — Mandatory, Non-Punitive, Individualized
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT Z-c: SUPPORTIVE MEASURES — MANDATORY, NON-PUNITIVE, INDIVIDUALIZED
PCHE Summer 2020 Title IX Training (Connelly)

Slide 38 — Supportive Measures Definition
New Definition, but familiar ground, as it codifies best practices:
- Available to both Complainants and Respondents
- Designed to restore or preserve equal access to the education program or activity without unreasonably burdening the other party, including measures designed to protect the safety of all parties or the educational environment, or deter sexual harassment
- The Title IX Coordinator is responsible for coordinating the effective implementation of supportive measures
- Fact specific inquiry — no unreasonable burden (some burden ok)

Slide 39 — Characteristics
Formerly called Interim Measures, these are school-provided services that are:
- Available at any time after a report of sexual harassment
- Non-disciplinary
- Non-punitive
- Not "unreasonably burdensome" to the other party
- Individualized
- Provided at no cost

PRACTICE POINTER: Do a word search for "Interim" on your webpages

Slide 40 — Examples
Familiar Supportive Measures include:
- Work or class schedule modifications
- Counseling
- Mutual no contact orders
- Changes in housing or work locations
- Leaves of absence
- Extensions of deadlines

Must be documented, and kept as confidential as possible

PRACTICE POINTER: Removal from teams, clubs, etc., are not Supportive Measures. Use your emergency removal process

exhibits.name: Conflict of Interest and Bias — Training Requirements
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT Z-d: CONFLICT OF INTEREST AND BIAS — TRAINING REQUIREMENTS
PCHE Summer 2020 Title IX Training (Connelly)

Slide 43 — Fairness, Equity, and Impartiality
The Title IX Team must identify and avoid:
- Bias against individuals or types of people
- Pre-judgment based on gender, race, sex, religion, disability, etc.
- Pre-judgment based on status as a Complainant or Respondent
- Conflicts of interest
- Profiling and predictive behaviors

When in doubt, remember to treat parties the same

Slide 44 — What is Bias?
Bias is a predisposition to think about a specific group and its individual members in a preconceived way
- Explicit: fully and clearly expressed or demonstrated
- Implicit: implied, rather than expressly stated; unconscious

Slide 45 — Avoiding Bias
- Learn about, be alert for, and avoid, stereotypes such as sex stereotypes
- Do not prejudge. Beware of making judgments or assumptions about the validity of the allegations even before you start your work or even mid-process
- Review and analyze all relevant evidence gathered
- Avoid leading questions
- Focus on the elements needed for proof
- Remember: you might not get credit for the right answer if you don't show your work (be prepared to justify your conclusions with the information you have gathered)

Slide 46 — What is a Conflict of Interest?
A conflict between the private interests and the official responsibilities of a person in a position of trust (Merriam-Webster)

COI must be avoided to:
- Protect process participants
- Ensure fairness
- Maintain the trust, integrity, and credibility of the Title IX process
- Comply with Title IX
- Avoid litigation and minimize appeals

Slide 47 — Avoiding COI
Examples of potential COI:
- Appeal Decision-Maker's spouse was a witness in the investigation
- Investigator accepts 50 yard-line tickets from Athletic Director
- Title IX Team member publishes a blog that stereotypes based upon gender

PRACTICE POINTER: In scanning for COI, consider where there may be influence unrelated to the evidence in the case, such as personal relationships, financial relationships, or power dynamics

Slide 48 — COI Response Best Practices
- Design a way for the parties and Title IX Team members to report bias and COI
- Encourage Title IX Team member to disclose potential bias and COIs
- Create written guidelines for assessing bias and COI stating that a determination will be based on objective facts, and not on generalizations, stereotypes, suspicions, or conclusions
- Use an objective standard for determinations like whether "based on objective facts, a reasonable person would believe that a COI exists"

exhibits.name: Actual Knowledge & Responsible Employee Program
exhibits.text:
EXHIBIT Z-e: ACTUAL KNOWLEDGE & RESPONSIBLE EMPLOYEE PROGRAM
PCHE Summer 2020 Title IX Training (Connelly)

Slide 30 — Actual Knowledge Defined
Actual knowledge means notice of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment to:
- A recipient's Title IX Coordinator; or
- Any official of the recipient who has authority to institute corrective measures on behalf of the recipient

Imputation of knowledge based solely on respondent being an employee is not enough

Slide 31 — What Triggers Actual Knowledge?
Any person may report sex discrimination, including sexual harassment, to the Title IX Coordinator. A report may be made at any time (including during non-business hours) by using the telephone number or electronic mail address, or by mail to the office address, listed for the Title IX Coordinator.

PRACTICE POINTER: How will your school receive reports? Ensure there are multiple ways to report and that each reporting mechanism is monitored.

Slide 32 — Who Has Authority?
The preamble states that a school's Title IX Coordinator, and any official with "authority to institute corrective measures" has actual knowledge.

This could include: Provost, VP for Student Affairs, Dean of Students, HR Director, etc.

PRACTICE POINTER: Consider whether your institution defines "authority to institute corrective measures" more broadly than just the Title IX Coordinator

Slide 33 — Responsible Employee Program
What if a school designates ALL employees as mandatory reporters? Then the school will have "actual knowledge" whenever any employee knows of sexual harassment or allegations of sexual harassment.

This is a policy decision each institution must make.

Slide 34 — Consequences of Responsible Employee Designation
If your institution has a responsible employee program:
- Every employee who receives a report = actual knowledge to the institution
- Failure to act on that knowledge = potential deliberate indifference
- Training ALL employees on reporting obligations is critical

PRACTICE POINTER: If you have a responsible employee program, make sure every employee knows what to do when they receive a report
